title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Here's today's research digest across your tracked topics — anti-aging, HIIT for women, pelvic floor training, alternative/targeted cancer therapy, and sleep. Each section summarizes recent, reputable findings; sources are linked as chips. Informational only — not medical advice.

sections.body:
The honest headline remains: **animal results haven't translated cleanly to humans yet.**

- **Rapamycin (sirolimus):** A 2025 *Aging-US* evidence review concluded there is **no clear clinical evidence** that low-dose, off-label rapamycin slows aging or extends healthspan in healthy adults — larger, better-designed trials are needed. The 2025 **RAPA-EX-01** RCT (weekly 6 mg sirolimus + home exercise in adults 65–85) found **no clear performance benefit** and flagged a **caution on short-term safety** (more infections in the treatment arm). The earlier PEARL trial did hint at sex-specific gains (lean mass in women), so the picture is mixed, not settled.
- **NAD+ boosters:** A 2026 randomized, placebo-controlled trial of a novel oral NAD+ formulation rapidly raised *intracellular* NAD within 5 days and was well tolerated — but the clinical signals are **hypothesis-generating only**, not proof of an anti-aging effect.

**Bottom line:** Favor interventions backed by human RCTs, and treat single-supplement "longevity" claims with healthy skepticism. The most consistent anti-aging levers remain unglamorous: exercise, sleep, and metabolic health.

sources.title: What is the clinical evidence to support off-label rapamycin therapy in healthy adults? (evidence review)
Exercise and Weekly Sirolimus (Rapamycin) in Older Adults: RAPA-EX-01 Randomised, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial
Human trials exploring anti-aging medicines (review of metformin, NAD+, senolytics, etc.)

sections.body:
HIIT keeps proving itself as a **time-efficient cardio booster** — but new evidence sharpens *what it does and doesn't do*.

- A **2026** systematic review & meta-analysis (19 RCTs) in middle-aged and older women found HIIT **significantly improves cardiorespiratory fitness (VO₂max; SMD 1.20, high certainty)** — but showed **no significant gains in muscle strength, flexibility, or sit-to-stand**. Translation: HIIT alone is **not enough** for overall functional fitness.
- A 2025 meta-analysis in recreationally active females confirmed HIIT raises VO₂max and **modestly lowers body-fat percentage**, with little change in total body mass.
- A randomized trial in young women with overweight found **HIIT + resistance training beats HIIT alone** for cardiorespiratory fitness and body composition.

**Practical takeaway:** Use HIIT for the engine (VO₂max), but **pair it with resistance and balance work** for strength, function, and long-term results. Intensity is the active ingredient; recovery and consistency make it stick.

sources.title: Effects of HIIT on cardiopulmonary fitness and physical function in middle-aged and elderly women: systematic review & meta-analysis
Effects of HIIT on physical fitness and body composition in recreationally active females: systematic review & meta-analysis
Combined HIIT and resistance training improves cardiorespiratory fitness more than HIIT alone in young women with overweight/obesity: RCT

sections.body:
**Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)** remains evidence-based first-line care — and 2026 data refines *how* to do it best.

- A **2026** *JAMA Network Open* multicenter RCT (764 first-time mothers across 9 hospitals) tested **PEFLOW** — PFMT plus global postural exercises started at 28 weeks' gestation. It **cut early postpartum stress incontinence** (8.7% vs 13.9% in controls at 6 weeks) and more than doubled the share of women with strong pelvic-floor function.
- A **2026** network meta-analysis (49 RCTs, 5,432 patients) ranked **PFMT + vaginal weights** highest for reducing leakage and building strength, while **PFMT + biofeedback + electrical stimulation** ranked best for quality of life.
- A **2026** trial of intensive *preoperative* PFMT in women awaiting incontinence surgery was striking: **43% postponed their planned surgery** after training.

**Key point:** *Technique and consistency beat sheer reps.* Correct activation (ideally confirmed with a clinician or biofeedback) — plus adjuncts like weighted cones — is what drives results.

sources.title: Pelvic Floor Workout (PEFLOW) for Preventing Stress Urinary Incontinence in Primiparous Women: a Randomized Clinical Trial
Effects of various exercise interventions for postpartum stress urinary incontinence: systematic review & network meta-analysis
Effectiveness of Intensive Preoperative Pelvic Floor Muscle Training in Women with Stress Urinary Incontinence Awaiting Surgery: an RCT

sections.body:
Big momentum in **HER2-positive breast cancer**, plus a promising lead for **HER2 + TP53 (p53) co-mutated** disease.

- **First-line standard is shifting.** The phase 3 **DESTINY-Breast09** trial (published in *NEJM*) showed the antibody-drug conjugate **trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) + pertuzumab** nearly doubled progression-free survival vs the long-standing THP regimen (~**40.7 vs 26.9 months**; HR 0.56). The FDA approved this first-line combination on **Dec 15, 2025**.
- **Earlier-stage news:** In 2026 the FDA also approved T-DXd (ENHERTU) for **neoadjuvant and adjuvant** early HER2+ breast cancer (DESTINY-Breast11 and -05).
- **p53 / TP53 angle:** p53 itself is still **"undruggable"** — no approved p53-targeted drug. But new preclinical work shows **HER2 + TP53 co-mutated** tumors (resistant to neratinib) stay **sensitive to T-DXd's TOP1-inhibitor payload**, and **neratinib + T-DXd** is being explored in an early-phase trial (NCT05372614).

**Framing:** "Alternative" should mean **evidence-based, oncologist-guided** options — complements to, not replacements for, standard care.

sources.title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan plus Pertuzumab for HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast09)
ENHERTU approved in the US for neoadjuvant and adjuvant HER2-positive early breast cancer (DESTINY-Breast11 & -05)
HER2; p53 Co-mutated Cancers Show Increased Histone Acetylation and are Sensitive to Neratinib plus Trastuzumab Deruxtecan

sections.body:
For restless or disjointed sleep, the evidence still favors **behavior over pills** — and increasingly, *accessible* behavioral therapy.

- **CBT-I is the gold standard.** A 2026 network meta-analysis in middle-aged/older adults found **CBT-I** gave the largest sleep-quality improvement (PSQI mean difference ≈ −6.3 vs usual care). **Meditative movement** (e.g., tai chi/yoga) emerged as the strongest *adjunct* (≈ −5.5), with acupuncture a more modest option.
- **Digital CBT-I works when you can't see a therapist.** A 2025 meta-analysis (29 RCTs, ~9,475 people) found fully-automated digital CBT-I delivers moderate-to-large benefits (therapist-guided is still best, and **adherence matters most**). A 2025 RCT of FDA-authorized **SleepioRx** showed gains **sustained at 6 months**.
- **Supplements are secondary.** Magnesium and melatonin are **not proven primary treatments** for chronic insomnia (magnesium shows only modest, often placebo-driven effects); melatonin is best reserved for circadian issues like jet lag/shift work.

**Habits that move the needle:**
1. Fixed **wake time**, even on weekends.
2. Bed for **sleep only**; keep the room cool, dark, quiet.
3. Dim lights and cut screens during a wind-down window.
4. Persistent problems → see a provider to rule out **sleep apnea** and consider **CBT-I**.

sources.title: Comparative Efficacy of Non-Pharmacological Adjuncts to CBT for Insomnia in Middle-Aged and Older Adults: a Network Meta-Analysis
Systematic review and meta-analysis on fully automated digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (29 RCTs)
Effectiveness of Digital CBT (SleepioRx) to Treat Insomnia Disorder in US Adults: Decentralized RCT
